items.title: Go woke, get rich: why rightwing hate campaigns don’t affect the box office
items.blurb:
After a bigoted online backlash, led by Elon Musk, The Odyssey achieved a monster weekend – the latest example of a targeted takedown that didn’t work

  • The Odyssey: jaw-dropping action, dark magic and dolphin cameos – discuss with spoilers

Elon Musk was a man on a mission. Yes, he’s supposedly intent on colonizing Mars, or the moon, or maybe, if you give him some more time to move the goalposts, just a really, really tall mountain, but this spring, a more pressing task presented itself, a sequel to his early-2026 mission to post white supremacist rhetoric: take down Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey, which he decided was the latest of “woke Hollywood’s” attempts to spread racial animus (towards white people, of course) and win Oscars while doing so. Nolan, Musk explained to the trade publication Variety, was “pissing on Homer’s grave”, referring to the author of the film’s epic-poem source material.

As the final phases of the movie’s promotional campaign ramped up, Musk posted dozens of times on his self-owned social media platform with grievances related to the casting of Lupita Nyong’o (a Black woman) and Elliot Page (a trans man) in the movie, often spreading false statements about this casting existing to satisfy new Oscar requirements. (As detailed here, those new Oscar diversity requirements are extremely flexible, and often satisfied by studio-wide programs that have nothing to do with casting. The Odyssey did not need to hire Oscar winner Nyong’o to be eligible for awards.) Musk may be a divisive-at-best figure who large swaths of the population do not take seriously, but he commands enough attention and adulation from a vocal minority that plenty of online accounts were soon parroting his willful misunderstanding of Hollywood and The Odyssey. Many of them abruptly and hilariously zeroed in on the 2004 movie Troy – which the right flank of social media suddenly decided was a beloved masterpiece, rather than its own, far greater distortion of Homer’s epic poem The Iliad.

Continue reading...

items.title: Pass the pit diapers! Why are fans wetting themselves to stand front row at concerts?
items.blurb:
Olivia Rodrigo, Noah Kahan and Morgan Wallen have all dealt with audience effluence in recent weeks – but that’s only one part of a fearsome subculture that has built up around fandom, endurance and ‘getting barrier’

The front row of a gig is a sacred space. When you love an artist, you want to be as close to them as possible, without a view obscured by someone else’s hairdo. You want the possibility that the artist will pluck you from the crowd, form a deep and enduring connection and end up marrying you. Or at least notice you.

Last month, Olivia Rodrigo revealed how acutely she had noticed some of the fans lining the barriers at her shows. “People wear diapers so that they can be front row,” she said, adding, to everyone’s absolute horror: “That’s been an experience as a performer that I have smelled.”

This is no aberration, and while genuine medical needs should be taken into account, situations down the front have clearly got out of control. Also last month, video of a very stoic stadium worker cleaning up human excrement at a Noah Kahan gig went viral and prompted Kahan himself to post on X: “If you have to poop at a show please dear god just go to the bathroom lmao.” This week, a fan of US country star Morgan Wallen complained that he, his brother and his niece were urinated on by another concertgoer, and police are investigating. Must we be subjected to headlines such as “Someone Also Shit Their Pants at a 5 Seconds of Summer Concert”? And in 2024, someone urinated on the floor of the standing section of a Sabrina Carpenter concert, causing a run on a fairly horrifying item called a “pit diaper”, whose mere existence speaks to a wider problem.

Continue reading...

items.blurb:
Ivy Meeropol’s engrossing film follows former star columnist E Jean Carroll, now in her 80s, battles through an unfinished series of legal disputes

Donald Trump has almost succeeded in an insidiously sneaky trick of auto-uncancelling on the #MeToo issue, implying that he is above and beyond and constitutionally exempt, despite being caught on tape boasting of sexual violence. Through a combination of bluster, bad faith, threats, abuse and the simple fact of being US president and flooding the zone on a grander international stage, and moreover with an aggressive army of online trolls at his disposal, he has got away with it. So far.

But among his accusers, the one who has so far been successful in two civil suits is the journalist and former advice columnist E Jean Carroll, showing remarkable courage and indomitable good humour. She is the subject of this engrossing documentary from film-maker Ivy Meeropol, co-produced by former Vanity Fair editor Graydon Carter, who incidentally has perhaps a few hail marys of his own to offer for having at first ducked the Jeffrey Epstein story. Carroll had an uproarious career in New York media in the 80s and 90s, in which she was a wisecracking Rosalind Russell-slash-Joan Rivers figure, creating a tough broad persona; she was in fact sometimes less than sympathetic to Paula Jones and Anita Hill in the Bill Clinton and Clarence Thomas cases. Carroll had a TV advice show called Ask E Jean (given to her, as she points out without comment, by the notorious exec Roger Ailes, himself an abuser brought low by #MeToo).

items.title: Venus, Vanishing by Rebecca Birrell review – art, desire and the rise of the Third Reich
items.blurb:
This portrait of a Jewish painter in bohemian Berlin is filled with insightful observations

In the summer of 1937, two exhibitions sponsored by the Nazis opened in Munich. For the Degenerate Art exhibition, hundreds of confiscated works by banned modern artists – including Paul Klee, Wassily Kandinsky, Pablo Picasso – were gathered: a derisive demonstration of the supposedly anti-German spirit of contemporary culture. The show was also meant as a counterpoint to the first Great German Art Exhibition, essentially an art fair for state-sanctioned work, which ran annually until 1944. The official style of Aryan art was traditionalist neoclassicism; popular subjects included idealised female nudes, pastoral landscapes and heroic battle scenes. Hitler was among the main buyers. You probably haven’t heard of any of those artists.

What if one of them were secretly a Jew, working under a pseudonym? Rebecca Birrell’s debut novel imagines this scenario, telling the story of a fictional Jewish artist who comes of age in Berlin in the last days of the Weimar republic. Hannah Sherman is working as a seamstress for the family business when she learns that she is about to be married off. She runs away, finding lodgings using the “desirable Christian” name of Schmidt, and begins to realise her artistic ambitions. A free-thinking New Woman, she also takes two lovers: Saul, a museum curator, and Charlotte, a dancer at a lesbian bar where Hannah likes to sketch.

items.title: A Toxic Love Story review – this astonishing real-life saga will leave you goggle-eyed and barely breathing

In a jaw-dropping return to true-crime form, Netflix’s new documentary delves into the Anaheim Craigslist case – and it’s a horrifying monster indeed

The sound of barrel-scraping during true-crime documentaries is becoming more and more frequent at Netflix these days (the dismal Worst Neighbor Ever being the most recent case in point). Such are the perils of being the pioneer of a form; you run out of material first but must keep trying to recapture past glories while you search desperately for another way to seize the fickle audience’s attention once again.

A Toxic Love Story is a welcome, if doubtless temporary, return to form. It is a rigorous, 88-minute recounting of an astonishing story that became notorious about 10 years ago in the US and was known as “the Anaheim Craigslist case” or “the Anaheim Craigslist rape fantasy case”, depending on just how many local Craigslist-based delinquencies were occurring at the exact time of reference. It has, after all, been the most criminally fertile of lists.

items.blurb:
Moments of beauty and horror punctuate this account of an ill-timed sub-zero camping trip

In late February 2022, Charlie Walker set off on a hiking holiday in northern Siberia. In temperatures as low as -50C, he intended to trek a thousand miles north along the frozen surface of the river Lena, then out across the solid Laptev Sea to the port town of Tiksi, before finally flying home to London. “The plan,” he writes, “was simple in concept.” A sub-zero camping trip through a region populated by wolves and bears and scarred by centuries of historical trauma, in a country that was about to invade one of its neighbours: what could possibly go wrong?

On Thin Ice is Walker’s third travelogue. Previous excursions have included a 43,000-mile bicycle ride across three continents, a 1,000-mile solo walk through the Gobi desert, and a river trip down the Congo in a leaky canoe. When not performing incredible feats of forward motion, according to his website, he gives talks to business leaders on subjects such as “risk taking” and “the line between courage and recklessness”. Reading his latest, it’s clear he’s familiar with the terrain on both sides of that line.

items.title: The world is on fire, and who does Trump blame? The Canadians | Arwa Mahdawi
items.blurb:
The US president has been complaining about Canada’s ‘filthy, polluted and unhealthy air’. Maybe he should put all that energy into tackling the climate crisis

Right, that’s it: we’ve got to do something about Canada. The country’s wildfires, which resulted in air quality alerts across more than 20 US states, ruined my weekend. On Friday the air in Philadelphia, where I live, was so unhealthy I couldn’t go outside without my eyes stinging. My kid’s preschool cancelled outdoor time, while the city suspended trash pickup and closed public pools.

You know who did not suspend their regularly scheduled programming because of a bit of toxic smoke? The plot monitoring committee at my allotment. Despite the apocalyptic conditions, they were still busy ensuring the gardening bylaws were strictly adhered to. I realised this on Saturday when I got an official violation for too many weeds. Yes, I’d got a bit carried away with “chaos gardening” (all the rage these days) and I’d meant to clean things up on Friday, until the smoke got in the way. So, thanks for that Canada.

Continue reading...

items.title: The Treasury will check Andy Burnham’s ambitions – unless old orthodoxies are challenged | Larry Elliott
items.blurb:
Fifty years ago, another Healey ran the Treasury. Today’s chancellor may find some things haven’t changed

Some things never change. Fifty years ago, Britain was sweltering in the long hot summer of 1976. The Labour government was acutely aware that the financial markets were scrutinising its every move. And a man called Healey was running the Treasury.

On the surface, the challenges facing John Healey are less severe than those Denis Healey had to grapple with half a century ago. Inflation is a lot lower and the City has reacted calmly to Andy Burnham as prime minister. And, while things could turn nasty pretty quickly, they would have to get a lot worse before Healey Mk II felt the need to ask the International Monetary Fund for an emergency loan.

items.title: It cost £2.1bn to divert a river in Brazil’s outback. So where has all the water gone?
items.blurb:
Despite official claims that the São Francisco River project supplies 12m people, many of those who had to make way for its construction in 2007 still have no water at all

A concrete canal cutting through the Caatinga scrubland feeds a surface reservoir two and a half miles from Maria de Fátima Ferreira da Silva’s home in rural Brazil. The canal passes Sertânia and continues until it reaches Monteiro, the next town along, where it spills its precious cargo into the bed of the Paraíba River – a human-directed inflow into the seasonally dry waterway.

Such proximity to a reliable water source would usually be considered a blessing in the sertão, a vast outback in Brazil’s north-east known for its punishing droughts. But not for Ferreira, who was displaced nearly 20 years ago by the construction of the canal and now lives in a house without any water supply, despite the nearby reservoir.

Maria de Fátima Ferreira da Silva in her maize crop at Salão productive rural village. She bathes at the reservoir and collects water there for her smallholding and donkey, and pays to fill her barrel with drinking water each month

In the first operation of its kind between a Premier League club and their local police authority, Liverpool’s investigations team worked with the force’s economic crime team to analyse almost 700,000 ticket registrations last season and to focus on the laundering of criminal proceeds from illicit ticket sales.

The joint investigation has led to £1.2m in assets related to organised ticket touts being detained with several cases taking place under the Proceeds of Crime Act. Two people have been convicted and another is due in court next month. The assets seized will be split between Merseyside police and the Home Office.

items.title: Football Daily | Chelsea’s financial shrug and Liverpool leaking players – what you may have missed
items.blurb:
Sign up now! Sign up now! Sign up now? Sign up now!

After the eye-watering excess of the Geopolitics World Cup, we can return to the modest, measured land of the domestic game. While our attention has been elsewhere, Manchester City have splashed £116m on Elliot Anderson. Chelsea, not to be outshone, have splurged £117m on Morgan Rogers. Even Tottenham have parted with £230m to revamp their mildly traumatised squad. Manchester United, who went down to Wrexham in their first pre-season friendly in Helsinki, have kept things modest, buying Andrey Santos and Youri Tielemans for the same £85m Spurs have thrown in the direction of relegation’s West Ham for Mateus Fernandes.

I was pleased with a lot of things that I saw in the game. We could do better. We could have taken more responsibility to have the ball instead of just watching and reacting … efficiency is very important at this level. When you don’t kill, you get killed” – new Hearts boss Wouter Vrancken chooses to focus on the positives after the, erm, 4-0 tonking by Sturm Graz in the first leg of their Bigger Cup qualifier.

How’s your World Cup hangover? I must have been bored because I couldn’t help but notice that Tuesday was a rough day for British, Irish and British Overseas Territory club teams in Bigger Cup. Not only were Hearts, Larne, Shamrock Rovers and Lincoln Red Imps all beaten in their second qualifying round first legs, they didn’t manage a single goal among them! Rovers’ Cape Verdean Pico Lopes went from taking Argentina to extra time in Miami just a few weeks ago, to getting blanked by Ararat in Armenia. Larne were were given no home comforts on the coast of Northern Ireland by Red Star Belgrade, who brought on Marko Arnautovic, the Austria veteran who retired from his national side after exiting the GWC at the hands of Spain. Football just rolls on and on” – Peter Oh.

Here we go again, another new face in the top job, you know the drill by now. Smiles, handshakes and social media soundbites. Claims of ‘understanding why people have been frustrated’, promises of ‘change’ and ‘progress’. Speculation about who’s being brought in to play a key role, and who’s out of favour. Sooner or later, the realisation there is no time or trust to make the difficult long-term changes everyone knows are necessary. Media pressure, infighting, the ‘need to act before it’s too late’. Out the door to be replaced by the next great hope, mainly distinguishable by having a different accent. All of which to say, good luck to Alessio Dionisi at Watford this season. Now to see what’s going on in UK politics …” – David Craddock.

items.title: New faces, gulfs in class and flawed drama: how 48 teams changed the World Cup
items.blurb:
The first 48-team World Cup had its positives and negatives – do we need to brace for a 64-team future?

It was less than 12 months after Gianni Infantino succeeded Sepp Blatter as president that Fifa announced the expansion of the men’s World Cup from 32 to 48 teams. The reaction was mixed. Diego Maradona said he was “delighted by Gianni’s initiative” because it allowed each country to dream of reaching the tournament, renewing their passion for football. Joachim Löw, then in charge of world champions Germany, said: “I don’t think it’s a good idea to dilute the sporting value … In the long term, the quality suffers.”

On the evidence of the first 48-team edition, won by Spain after 104 games across five-and-a-half weeks in North America, it’s fair to conclude Maradona and Löw both had a point.

items.title: A tournament that crossed borders: crunching the numbers on the diaspora World Cup
items.blurb:
Nationality in football has never been so complex and the influence of players from the diaspora has never been higher at a World Cup

“Nationality says where you come from. And I actually come from four countries: France, Algeria, Nigeria and Great Britain. I consider myself very lucky to possess these four parts, which all enrich me.” This view was given by Michael Olise. He set up seven goals at the World Cup, breaking Pelé’s record for the most assists at a single edition of the tournament. And he did so for France, one of four countries he was eligible to represent, but not the one in which he was born.

The influence of players from the diaspora has never been higher at the World Cup. Four years ago, 16.5% of the players at the tournament were born in different nations from those for whom they played. That represented a 5.1% increase on 2018 and was the previous high for the tournament. No longer. Almost a quarter (23.6%) of the 1,248 players named in squads this year were selected by countries other than their birthplace. This proportion remained broadly consistent for the contribution these players made towards the minutes played, assists provided and goals scored. In a 48-team, 104-match tournament, these are big numbers.

items.title: As Dodgers head to White House, loyal fans are conflicted: ‘Trump is tearing this city apart’
items.blurb:
After ICE raids and protest crackdown, the LA team’s supporters are ambivalent about the World Series winners’ visit

Like many children of Latino immigrants in Los Angeles, Juan grew up a Dodgers fan. He watched their baseball games from home when he was little – even though his parents, who are Guatemalan, were more interested in soccer and basketball – and has been to Dodger Stadium many times as an adult. He sports a Dodgers logo, among other symbols, on an elaborate tattoo on his left forearm.

These days, though, he’s not sure about his allegiance. Juan – whom the Guardian is only identifying by his first name amid the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown – saw his family ripped apart when his father, who had been in the US for close to 30 years, was plucked off the street last year and deported. From that moment on, a team that many immigrants in LA see as a symbol of integration and belonging became, for him, an organization unwilling to stand up for its large immigrant fanbase.

items.title: World Cup 2030 (very early) power rankings: who will challenge Spain at top?
items.blurb:
The next men’s World Cup is still four years away, but we take a look at the teams with the firmest foundations who should be primed to contend

The 2026 World Cup provided many an esteemed veteran with what could be their international swansongs, with golden greats Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo, Luka Modric, Neymar, James Rodríguez and Guillermo Ochoa all making teary exits as their tournament ended. As we turn ahead to the 2030 World Cup, any player 32 or older will be sweating their selection status and changing their daily routines to stay sharp against inevitable aging.

For these power rankings, we’re overvaluing sure things. Teams with plenty of leading players comfortably in their 20s can build for this impending four-year cycle with greater confidence than a team replacing an ageless talisman. Consider these the 10 teams with the firmest foundations and viable reasons to hope to reach a quarter-final in four years’ time.

items.title: Why many Black Americans were rooting for Argentina to lose the World Cup | Etan Thomas
items.blurb:
Many Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with Argentina

There are moments in sports when you realize people aren’t just watching the score any more. They’re watching values. They’re watching who speaks. They’re watching who stays silent. And they’re watching what those silences say.

I found myself thinking about that throughout this World Cup. On paper, Sunday’s final looked simple. Spain v Argentina. Two footballing giants and countries with glorious soccer histories. Two nations whose pasts are also stained by colonization, oppression and chapters of history that shouldn’t be whitewashed simply because someone can score a goal.

items.title: Meet Two Engineers Building a 1,000-Km Network to Improve Our Cyber Security and Protect India’s Data
items.blurb: A bill is paid. A medical report is opened. A document is shared. A few taps on a phone are all it takes to pay a bill, transfer money , access a health report, or share an important document. It all feels effortless. These moments take only seconds, and most of us never stop to wonder how this information remains private. We trust that the invisible systems protecting our data will continue to do their job. Still, behind this everyday convenience lies a growing concern that has kept scientists and cybersecurity experts awake for years. The encryption that protects today’s digital world may not always be enough. As quantum computers move from research laboratories towards reality, they could one day crack many of the security systems the world currently depends upon. Nearly a decade ago, when this conversation was still confined to academic circles, Sunil Gupta and Srinivasa Rao Aluri were already asking themselves a genuine question. If India was racing towards becoming one of the world’s largest digital economies, who was preparing to secure that future? That belief led them to establish ‘QNu Labs’ in 2016. The company was incubated at IIT Madras in Tamil Nadu before moving its headquarters to Bengaluru, Karnataka, where it continues to build technologies designed to protect India’s digital future. Around 10 years later, it has grown into what the founders describe as India’s only full-stack quantum cybersecurity company. This means it builds the hardware, software, and secure applications needed to protect communication systems across defence, banking, government, and critical infrastructure. Looking back, Sunil, the co-founder and CEO of the organisation, says the idea was never about predicting the future. It was about preparing for it. “When we began, quantum computing wasn’t something people discussed outside research circles, but one question kept coming back to us. If India’s digital economy is growing this fast, shouldn’t we start preparing for the risks that could come with it?” he tells The Better India. Preparing before the threat arrived In 2016, quantum computers were still largely confined to laboratories. For many people, the technology sounded like something from science fiction rather than an immediate concern. Friends, investors, and industry experts frequently questioned why a young Indian startup wanted to spend years solving a problem that sounded so far away. The company was incubated at IIT Madras in Tamil Nadu before moving its headquarters to Bengaluru, Karnataka. The founders heard those doubts routinely. “People told us we were working on tomorrow’s problem,” recalls Srinivasa. “We looked at it differently. If you wait until tomorrow arrives, you have already lost valuable time. Security has to be built before the threat becomes real.” It was a philosophy that drove everything the company did. Most cybersecurity companies were focused on identifying attacks faster or responding more efficiently once systems had already been compromised. The duo wanted to approach the problem from a completely different direction. Instead of improving the alarm system, they wanted to redesign the lock itself . “We believed security needed an urgent shift from ‘early detection and faster response’ to proactive immunity,” says Sunil. “For us, the goal was not only to react better. It was to make attacks substantially harder in the first place.” That decision meant that for years, there would be no quick payoff. The founders had to sit with the uncertainty, keep building in labs, and explain the same idea to people who still saw it as too far away. But they believed that if the groundwork was done early, India would have a stronger chance of meeting the quantum era before it arrived at its doorstep. The science behind safe communication For many people, the word quantum immediately sounds scary. It brings to mind complicated equations, research laboratories, and concepts that appear impossible to understand. Yet the challenge QNu Labs is addressing can be explained through something surprisingly familiar. Imagine sending a valuable document inside a locked box. Both the sender and the receiver have identical keys, and only those keys can open the lock. As long as no one else gains access to the key, the document remains secure. That is basically how digital encryption works today. Every online payment, confidential email, or government record depends on secret digital keys that scramble information into unreadable code before it is transmitted across a network. As India embraced a digital future, Sunil Gupta (L) and Srinivasa Rao Aluri (R) focused on keeping it secure. Today’s encryption depends on problems that normal computers find extremely hard to solve. Quantum computers could solve some of them much faster, which means the same protection may become weaker in the future. QNu Labs’ platform tries to reduce that risk by using the laws of physics along with mathematics, making the security harder to break with computing power alone. Its flagship technology, Armos, uses Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) to create encryption keys without ever sending those keys across the communication network. Think of two people somehow agreeing on the same secret password without ever saying it aloud, writing it down, or sending it to each other. Since the password never actually travels, there is nothing for anyone else to intercept. Even more interestingly, the technology can immediately detect if somebody attempts to eavesdrop. “People usually assume quantum technology is too complicated to understand, but we like to explain it through something familiar. Imagine sharing a secret without ever saying it aloud. That’s mainly what quantum key distribution allows us to do, and if someone tries to listen in, you know immediately,” says Srinivasa. Another technology developed by the company is Tropos, a Quantum Random Number Generator, which creates genuinely unpredictable random numbers that are used to generate secure encryption keys. Regular computers follow a set pattern to create random numbers, which can sometimes be predicted. But quantum processes create randomness from nature itself, making the numbers much harder to guess. “Think of it as the difference between a magician’s trick and a coin toss. A trick follows a pattern that can be figured out, but something created by nature can be completely unpredictable,” he continues. The company has also built post-quantum cryptography, a newer form of encryption designed to keep data safe even when quantum computers become powerful enough to challenge today’s security systems. Together, these innovations form QShield, an integrated platform that combines hardware, software, and secure applications into one ecosystem. Importantly, organisations do not need to rebuild their existing networks to use them. “Most organisations have spent years building their digital infrastructure, and they don’t want to replace everything overnight. We aimed to create technology that works with existing systems while helping them prepare for the future,” adds Sunil. That practical approach soon took QNu Labs out of the laboratory and into environments where secure communication is not just desirable but central. When the technology moved beyond the lab One of the company’s earliest breakthroughs came in the high-altitude region of Leh, where it successfully generated quantum-safe encryption keys across a 300-kilometre optical fibre network under demanding conditions. The achievement proved that the technology could perform reliably beyond carefully controlled research environments. For the founders, it was more than a technical milestone. It was the first indication that years of research were beginning to translate into real-world impact. It also caught the attention of one of the country’s most demanding customers. On 14 August 2022, the Indian Army placed its first procurement order for the platform’s Quantum Key Distribution systems under the Innovations for Defence Excellence (iDEX) programme. Instead of improving the alarm system, they wanted to redesign the lock itself. Photograph: (Image generated by AI) For the duo, it was a moment that affirmed a belief they had held since the company began, that technology conceived, designed and built in India could protect some of the country’s most sensitive communications. “Receiving the army’s confidence was one of those moments that reminded us why we started,” says Sunil. “The achievement was a sign that homegrown innovation could meet the highest standards of security and safeguard some of the country’s most critical communications.” The recognition did not stop there. Within the following year, the Indian Navy placed a landmark order for 28 quantum encryption systems, making it one of the largest quantum security procurements in the country’s defence sector. It signalled that quantum-safe communication was no longer confined to research papers or pilot projects. It was beginning to find a place in India’s operational defence networks. For a young company that had spent years refining its technology, the army and navy orders were important milestones. But for the founders, they were never the destination. They were proof that quantum security could work where the stakes were highest. The larger ambition had always been to build a security framework that could eventually protect every sector relying on digital communication, from financial institutions and public infrastructure to healthcare and telecommunications. A 500-km trial took quantum security out of the lab The opportunity to illustrate that vision came soon afterwards. Working with the Indian Army’s Southern Command Signals, the organisation helped set up India’s first quantum secure communication channel across more than 500 kilometres of existing optical fibre in Rajasthan. The test ran on the army’s own communication backbone, with the fibre infrastructure provided by the Corps of Signals. Instead of laying an entirely new network, QNu Labs used the system already in place. Through multiple communication points, including two trusted points that helped pass the keys safely, the company securely exchanged quantum encryption keys across the network. Until then, many quantum communication experiments had remained inside laboratories or worked only across shorter fibre links. This test showed that India could begin preparing for the quantum era by strengthening the networks it had already spent decades building. “Every milestone has taken years of testing, learning, and refining. Completing the 500-kilometre quantum secure connection was not about setting a record. It showed that this technology could move beyond research labs and work on the networks people rely on every day,” says Srinivasa. QNu Labs’ platform tries to reduce that risk by using the laws of physics along with mathematics, making the security harder to break with computing power alone. Photograph: (Image generated by AI) The achievement also supported the vision of the National Quantum Mission, which aims to create indigenous quantum technologies across computing, sensing and secure communication. As India builds its quantum capabilities, the focus is now shifting from experiments in laboratories to finding ways these technologies can solve real-world challenges. The momentum continued. In April 2026, the startup completed another landmark achievement by establishing India’s first 1,000-kilometre Quantum Key Distribution network, the longest of its kind in the country. Running entirely over existing telecom fibre, the network maintained 99.3 percent uptime over 15 continuous days and was independently validated by government authorities along with VIAVI Solutions using its MAP-300 platform. For the founders, it was the culmination of nearly a decade of patient work. “When we started, quantum security sounded like a conversation for the future,” he says. “Today, we are seeing that future arrive much faster than many expected. We are glad we chose to prepare early rather than wait for the threat to become real.” Beyond defence, towards everyday digital life Although defence projects often attract the most attention, Sunil believes the real outcome of quantum security lies elsewhere. Every day, millions of people transfer money through banking apps, make digital payments, share medical records, file taxes online, or access government services. Behind every one of those activities lies encryption that people trust without a second thought. The challenge is guaranteeing that this trust continues even in a future led by quantum computing. One of the biggest concerns among cybersecurity experts today is a strategy known as ‘harvest now, decrypt later’. Instead of trying to crack encrypted information immediately, attackers can simply steal and store sensitive data today, waiting until quantum computers become powerful enough to unlock it years later. Financial records, defence communications, confidential business information, or government files intercepted now could all become vulnerable in the future. Preparing for that possibility is where QNu Labs sees its role. Its integrated platform, QShield, is already being deployed across public sector organisations, banks, payment networks and operators of critical infrastructure. Rather than forcing organisations to discard years of investment in existing systems, the platform works alongside current infrastructure, combining Quantum Key Distribution, Quantum Random Number Generation and post-quantum cryptography to strengthen security in a practical and scalable way. “Defence was never the final destination. The same technology that protects military communication can also secure banking systems, payment networks, hospitals, and public infrastructure. That is where its real value lies,” Sunil says. This ability to adapt to different sectors has helped the company move from pilot projects into real-world deployments, slowly becoming part of India’s growing cybersecurity ecosystem. Building products and patents in India For the duo, building products was only one part of the work. They also wanted India to have the ability to create, control, and improve such technologies on its own. Another technology developed by the company is Tropos, a Quantum Random Number Generator, which creates genuinely unpredictable random numbers that are used to generate secure encryption keys. For years, many advanced cybersecurity tools used to protect critical infrastructure have come from outside the country. These systems have played an important role, but when national security is involved, relying too heavily on foreign technology can become a concern. QNu Labs wanted to show that high-level quantum security could be designed, engineered, and manufactured in India. That commitment can be seen in its growing intellectual property portfolio. Today, the company has 12 granted patents, 15 patents awaiting grant, and more than 30 additional patent applications, giving it a strong base in quantum security innovation. Technologies such as Armos, Tropos, and their quantum secure communication platforms have all been developed by Indian engineers. “Building indigenous technology goes beyond replacing imports,” the co-founder says. “It gives us greater control over the systems that protect its most important information and creates capabilities that stay within the country.” The founders also recognise that building an industry requires people as much as products. Through QNu Academy, the company is helping train students, engineers, and researchers in quantum technologies, creating a talent pool for a field that is still in its early stages in India. “Technology alone does not build an ecosystem. You need researchers, engineers, and young innovators who are excited to take it forward. That is why we felt it was important to invest in developing talent alongside building products,” says Srinivasa. Apart from these efforts, they have continued to collaborate with government agencies, research institutions, and industry partners, contributing to the country’s broader ambition of becoming self-reliant in advanced technologies. The invisible trust behind a digital nation The strongest technology is often the one people never notice. When someone pays for groceries using a QR code, checks their bank balance on a mobile phone or sends confidential information across a secure network, they are unlikely to think about encryption, quantum physics or cybersecurity. Nor should they have to. Behind those everyday interactions lies a vast digital infrastructure that depends on trust. As that infrastructure grows, so too does the responsibility of protecting it against threats that may seem distant today and are still moving closer. The company has also built post-quantum cryptography, a newer form of encryption designed to keep data safe even when quantum computers become powerful enough. Nearly 10 years ago, Sunil and Srinivasa decided to prepare for that future before most people believed it had arrived. Since then, the company they built has gone from an idea incubated at IIT Madras to securing communications for the Indian Army and Navy, demonstrating quantum secure networks stretching over 1,000 kilometres, supporting banks and public institutions, and helping strengthen India’s indigenous quantum ecosystem. It is about recognising that trust is the foundation of a digital nation and quietly working to guarantee that trust remains intact for generations to come. As Sunil reflects, “The strongest security is the kind that works silently in the background. If people can make a payment, access a government service, or communicate securely without thinking about the systems protecting them, then we have done our job.”

items.title: Delhi Couple’s Home Stays Cool Without AC. How One Video Took It From Social Media To National Media
items.blurb: A home that stays more than 10 degrees cooler than Delhi’s scorching summer heat, without an air conditioner — sounds almost unbelievable. That’s exactly why Anita and Vijay’s story caught people’s attention. When The Better India shared a glimpse into their thoughtfully designed home, viewers weren’t just fascinated by the cooler temperatures. They were drawn to a larger idea: that everyday homes can be built to work with nature instead of constantly battling it. Within days, the story travelled far beyond social media feeds. Messages poured in, conversations began, and a decades-old experiment in sustainable living suddenly found a much larger audience. A home built for Delhi’s climate Nearly four decades ago, while Delhi was steadily filling up with concrete structures, Vijay’s father designed a home around the city’s climate . Instead of relying on energy-intensive cooling systems, the house used simple yet effective principles: a central courtyard open to the sky, cross ventilation, insulated ceilings, air ducts, and cool Kota stone flooring. The result is a home that remains significantly cooler than the outside temperature, even during peak summer. What started as one couple’s mindful lifestyle turned into a wider movement for climate-conscious homes. The sustainability journey extends beyond architecture. On the terrace, Anita and Vijay nurture more than 200 plants, growing herbs, fruits, and vegetables that form a substantial part of their meals. Through Khwabghar, Anita’s terrace kitchen studio, she hosts cooking workshops using ingredients harvested directly from her garden. Their lifestyle is equally intentional. Kitchen waste is composted and returned to the soil, rainwater is harvested to recharge groundwater, solar panels help meet energy needs, and much of the furniture inside the home has been reused or repurposed. Watch the original story that brought Anita and Vijay’s home to thousands here . View this post on Instagram A post shared by The Better India (@thebetterindia) When curiosity turned into conversation After the story was shared on The Better India’s Instagram English page, Anita began receiving calls and messages from people across her personal and professional circles. Students, friends, family members, and well-wishers reached out to share their appreciation and curiosity. Many wanted to learn more about the cooling techniques used in the house, terrace gardening, composting, and other sustainable practices featured in the story. For Anita and Vijay, the response was particularly meaningful because it showed that people were not just watching the story; they were engaging with the ideas behind it. Beyond walls and roofs, Anita and Vijay’s home became a reminder that simple ideas can create lasting change. The ripple effect continued when prominent media outlets such as Aaj Tak and Economic Times featured the couple and their work, helping take their message to an even wider audience. A personal lifestyle choice had become part of a larger public conversation on climate-smart housing. Watch how Anita and Vijay’s sustainable home reached a wider audience here . One house, many possibilities Anita and Vijay never set out to build a model home for the world. They simply chose to live differently. Yet their story shows how practical solutions that are sustained over decades can inspire far more people than we imagine.

items.title: The Auto Driver in Tamil Nadu Who Spent Most Of His Earnings in Planting 1 Lakh+ Trees in 10 Years
items.blurb: For most people walking past the temple in Sangagiri, it was just another tree. But for N Palanisamy, the banyan beside it had shaded entire chapters of his life, from childhood afternoons spent playing under its canopy to the comfort of passing it every day as an adult. In 2013, that tree was cut down to make way for the temple's expansion. A loss that changed everything Palanisamy, now 55 and known locally as the 'tree driver' of Sangagiri, has spoken about how deeply the feeling affected him. The same year also brought the death of environmentalist Dr G Nammalvar, whose writings and radio talks on living in harmony with nature had shaped Palanisamy's thinking for years. He had studied only up to Class 10, but Nammalvar's ideas stayed with him long after their one meeting. Losing both the tree and the man who inspired his outlook on nature within months of each other pushed him to act. He began planting saplings on the grounds of his old school that same year. To fund this, Palanisamy used part of his daily earnings, roughly Rs 250, from driving an auto rickshaw. It was money he could have kept for his household, spent instead on seeds and saplings for a cause that offered him no income in return. Planting is only half the job Palanisamy soon learnt that putting a sapling in the ground was the easy part. Protective cages, bamboo supports, nets, and regular watering were what determined whether a young tree survived cattle, weather, and neglect. Members of Pasumai Sangagiri, the volunteer group from Sangagiri, come together every Sunday to undertake tree conservation and environmental protection activities. Much of his money went into this unglamorous maintenance work rather than the saplings themselves. His wife, Gomathi, stood by him through these early years, encouragement that he credits as essential to keeping the effort alive. This instinct, that survival matters more than the act of planting, echoes a challenge that groups distributing free saplings across India, like an auto rickshaw driver in Kolhapur who has given away over 8,000 saplings , have also had to reckon with over the years. From one man to a movement Three years in, Palanisamy's persistence caught the attention of the Sangagiri Lorry Owners Association. When they offered help, he asked not for money but for protective cages, a request that led to a steady supply of cages and free water tankers for large-scale watering. In 2016, friends and nature enthusiasts who had followed his work on Facebook and WhatsApp came together to form Pasumai Sangagiri, a volunteer group that has since grown to 25 active members. Every Sunday, while much of the town rests, this group gathers at schools, roadsides, temples, and hospitals to water trees, clear weeds, and plant new saplings, an approach not unlike that of a Thane auto driver who has spent years watering roadside trees using cans carried in his rickshaw . When asked about his biggest achievement, Palanisamy does not reach for numbers. He speaks instead of children eating fruit from trees he once protected as saplings Even the pandemic could not stop the group entirely; when plantation drives paused, members turned to sanitising streets and residential areas instead. The scale of a quiet, decade-long effort Through a nursery Palanisamy runs, Pasumai Sangagiri has distributed more than 1 lakh saplings free of cost across Tamil Nadu. Volunteers have sown over 50,000 palm seeds along lakes, ponds, and irrigation canals, a method of low-maintenance greening similar to what has worked for a Pune resident who converted a parking lot into a free nursery over 25 years. The group has also intervened to save mature trees marked for felling during development projects, carefully replanting around 10 large trees, some over 50 years old, using cranes and heavy equipment, and monitoring them until they recovered. At Vadugapatti and Sangagiri Model School, campuses that were once bare now stand transformed by the group's efforts, a shift that mirrors what environmentalists devoted to protecting ancient trees often describe as the deeper, generational value of a single surviving tree. When asked about his biggest achievement, Palanisamy does not reach for numbers. He speaks instead of children eating fruit from trees he once protected as saplings, and of barren stretches now filled with greenery. More than a decade after losing a banyan tree that shaped his childhood, his Sunday afternoons still belong to the trees he continues to grow. Images courtesy of TNIE Source: ' Meet N Palanisamy, the tree driver of Tamil Nadu's Sangagiri ': by Sneha Sivashanmugam for The New Indian Express, Published on 5 July 2026

items.title: Handwritten Punjab Registers Help Add 9909 Indian Soldiers to World War I Records
items.blurb: For generations, some families held on to little more than fragments of memory. A great-grandfather who had left for the First World War and never returned. A faded photograph tucked away in a trunk. Stories passed down at family gatherings about a soldier whose sacrifice was remembered at home, but nowhere else. For many, those memories never found a place in the official record. More than a century after the guns of the First World War fell silent, that has finally begun to change. The Commonwealth War Graves Commission (CWGC) has officially recognised 9,909 Indian soldiers whose deaths had never been recorded in its First World War casualty records. The breakthrough came through an extraordinary research project centred on 34 handwritten Punjab Registers preserved at the Lahore Museum, an archive that helped recover the identities of thousands of servicemen from pre-Partition India, many of them from Punjab, whose wartime sacrifice had remained unrecorded for over 100 years. Together, the volumes documented nearly 3.2 lakh Punjabi recruits who served in the British Indian Army during the war. Their rediscovery has helped restore the names, identities and sacrifices of soldiers who had remained absent from official commemoration for more than 100 years. A discovery that began with 34 handwritten books The breakthrough can be traced back to 2014, when historian and author Amandeep Madra, co-founder of the UK Punjab Heritage Association, came across the Punjab Registers while researching military history. As he told The Tribune , historians had long known such registers existed, but few expected to find them inside an art museum. The significance became personal almost immediately. Punjab was the main recruiting-ground for the British in India during the First World War, with volunteer-rates in some Punjabi villages reaching 40%. Photograph: (Wikimedia Commons) One of the earliest registers Madra photographed belonged to Ambala district, where his own family hailed from. There, he found the name of his father's uncle, Bishen Singh, the only man from their village known to have served during the war. "My dad remembered being taken to collect his pension as a boy and that he'd been blinded by sandstorms during the war. That was all we knew. Then I found him in the register," Madra recalled in the interview. If one family could recover its history through the registers, he realised, thousands of others might be able to do the same. Piecing together lives, one name at a time The work that followed extended far beyond simply reading old documents. More than 26,000 fragile pages first had to be photographed, digitised, indexed and transcribed. The project eventually evolved into a five-year collaboration between the Commonwealth War Graves Commission, the UK Punjab Heritage Association and the University of Greenwich. Researchers, historians, volunteers and military experts painstakingly compared records from the Punjab Registers against approximately 74,000 existing CWGC Indian Army casualty records. It was painstaking work. Names had often been recorded differently by different clerks. Villages appeared under multiple spellings. Punjabi naming customs required careful interpretation, while service records from the colonial period frequently varied in detail. Heaviest recruitment during the war was from the Rawalpindi, Jalandhar, and Hoshiarpur districts of British Punjab. Photograph: (BBC/Lahore Museum) During the COVID-19 lockdown , volunteers began cross-checking soldiers listed in the registers as having martyred during the war. As more records were examined, a striking pattern emerged. Ultimately, researchers reviewed 15,935 recorded deaths, using computer-assisted analysis alongside manual verification. The result was the identification of 9,909 soldiers whose names had never entered the official record of wartime commemoration. Restoring names, restoring family history "Over a century after the end of the First World War, our mission endures, ensuring all those who died in the service of the Commonwealth receive the commemoration they deserve," said Claire Horton, Director General of the CWGC. "The Punjab Registers project is a landmark moment in that mission. The recovery of every one of these 9,909 names helps restore missing chapters in family and world histories. It stands as a constant reminder that commemoration is not only about the past — it is about personal identity, family legacy and recognising the human cost of war." Horton added that the Commission is working with Commonwealth governments to explore a permanent memorial that would honour these soldiers with the dignity they deserve. Filling one of the last gaps in World War I history More than 13 lakh Indians served during the First World War, making the British Indian Army one of the largest volunteer forces in history. Soldiers fought across Europe, Africa and the Middle East, from Flanders and Gallipoli to Mesopotamia and East Africa. Punjab supplied nearly half a million recruits, representing Sikh, Muslim, Hindu and Christian communities. The Punjab Registers have therefore become much more than military documents. They preserve details of villages, families and communities that existed before Partition, offering an extraordinary social record alongside military history. The work also forms part of the CWGC's wider Non-Commemoration Programme, launched in 2021 to identify individuals whose wartime service had never been formally recognised. So far, the programme has identified more than 20,000 additional names for commemoration. For the families of the 9,909 soldiers now added to the record, the announcement does not rewrite history. It completes it. After more than a century, names once confined to fading handwritten registers have entered the historical record they always belonged in, ensuring that the men who left home to serve are remembered as individuals whose stories are part of history. Sources: ‘Thousands of forgotten World War I Indian soldiers commemorated in UK’ : by Aditi Khanna, Published on 6 July 2026 ‘The forgotten 9,909: 34 Punjab Registers document sacrifice of unrecorded Indian soldiers who fought in World War 1’ : by Shyam Bhatia, Published on 12 July 2026

items.title: Engineer Turns 15000 Rural Kids Into Innovators With Free Computer & Coding Classes
items.blurb: Originally reported and written in August 2022, this story has been republished as part of our archival content. “How many of you have used a computer?” The answer to this question from class 7 students surprised Shoaib Dar, a fellow of Teach For India at the time. The engineer-turned-educator couldn’t believe that only one of them had used this electronic device. To equip his students for the digital world, Shoaib conducted a 10-day boot camp with four Raspberry-Pi computers, which cost Rs 4,000-5,000. Students created their own games and animations as well as came up with programmed solutions for everyday problems. This incident prompted Shoaib to begin the Pi Jam Foundation in 2017, an NGO aiming to promote digital literacy among rural children . The organisation has so far provided 400 computers for 15,500 kids in 51 schools around Telangana, Maharashtra and Jammu & Kashmir. One of the innovations of the students is a weather monitor checking the parameters such as humidity, temperature, rainfall, and wind speed. The collected data from this device is now being used by the Indian Meteorological Society in Pune. The 31-year-old said , “Children feel that computers are these complex devices that control them. I want them to understand that it is actually the other way around. Also, I did not want them to feel that working with computers is a hobby or an extracurricular activity. I want them to see the potential of learning these skills and how they can be participants in shaping the changing digital world.” Listen to the inspiring story of Shoaib and his NGO here: Edited by Yoshita Rao

items.title: This Duo Turned 10 Acres of Dry Land Into an Eco-Stay With 6000 Plants, Mud Walls & Local Jobs
items.blurb: “The first thing we did after buying it was nothing,” recalls Ankit Rastogi. For about three years, he and Pradeep Vijayan left the 10 acres they had bought near Kanha National Park largely untouched. The semi-barren land in Madhya Pradesh had fewer than 300 trees when they purchased it in December 2015. Today, the same protected stretch holds more than 6,000 native plants and trees, 59 bird species and a man-made pond that draws wildlife during summers, when water becomes scarce across the region. Ankit and Pradeep had spent decades in India’s travel industry and stayed in almost every kind of property imaginable. Through those years, they often saw tourism serve visitors while offering limited benefits to the surrounding communities and ecosystems. They wanted to build a different model near one of India’s best-known conservation areas. “We did not want to build something that looked responsible from the outside but operated like every other property on the inside,” says Ankit. Surwahi Social Ecoestate was conceived as a long-term impact venture where tourism could help restore the land, support local people and preserve traditional skills. Surwahi’s reception uses mud walls and furniture sourced second-hand or made locally The two founders continued with their corporate careers while building it. Ankit currently serves as the Chief Product Officer at Angel One, while Pradeep leads Hotels Business and Partnerships at Ixigo. Their corporate incomes funded and sustained Surwahi through its long development period, including the pandemic years. For its first 10 years, Pradeep worked as an active co-founder involved in every stage of building and running the estate. Ankit shaped the environmental vision and overall direction, while Pradeep led its execution on the ground. He handled inbound sales and marketing, business development, sustainability partnerships, digital distribution and content, as well as staff onboarding, training and engagement. His relationships with the local administration and the Forest and Revenue departments also helped the project find its footing. The estate’s main buildings use thick mud walls and recycled terracotta roof tiles. The estate was being built by two outsiders, and the idea initially faced scepticism in the area. Over the past year, Pradeep has stepped back from daily operations, although he remains involved as a founding promoter. What happens when you let nature be? The land, locally recorded as Satkata Jungle, began recovering after the founders fenced it. The fencing stopped illegal tree felling and blocked vehicles involved in illegal sand mining along the neighbouring Sandook River. Tractors and lorries had previously moved through the land, damaging the roots of existing trees and plants. Within two to three monsoons, the forest began regenerating naturally. Around 4,500 of Surwahi’s 6,000 plants and trees emerged through natural regeneration. The team also dug a man-made pond called Arjun Talab to collect monsoon run-off and recharge the groundwater. This reduced the amount of rainwater flowing unused into the Sandook River. Before drilling a borewell, they commissioned a hydrogeological survey to map the aquifer and identify the appropriate location. The survey also recommended catchment trenches to retain run-off and native species suited to the available water. These included Paras Peepal, Cassia siamea and local bamboo. The findings guided the planting of around 1,000 trees. Of the more than 6,000 plants and trees now growing on the estate, roughly 1,500 were planted. The remaining 4,500 emerged through natural regeneration. As the forest recovered, bees, birds and butterflies returned in greater numbers. Their pollinating activity supported further growth with every monsoon. More recently, the team added around 300 fruit and flowering varieties within the main compound. Once the land had begun recovering, Ankit and his team spent years looking for architects who shared their values. They eventually found Mrinmayee Architects from Bangalore, led by eminent professors from IISc and a young architect who worked with local masons to bring the vision to life. Workers from surrounding villages built the estate, from masonry to painting and plumbing. The construction brief was simple and uncompromising. The team wanted to avoid baked red bricks, glass and steel wherever possible. Thick mud walls went up instead, helping the buildings respond naturally to changing temperatures. The roofs were layered with recycled terracotta tiles collected from broken village homes nearby. “Every single worker, from the mason to the painter and plumber, came from the surrounding villages. That was non-negotiable,” says Ankit. “This land belongs to these people. The least we could do was make sure they built it.” Every piece of furniture was either bought second-hand from Jabalpur’s Gurundi market or made by local bamboo craftspeople. The ramp forms part of Surwahi’s facilities for older people and guests with disabilities. The first space, Maikal Express, opened in October 2019. The eight-bed earthen dormitory was named after the Maikal hills near Kanha and designed as a “co-living in the wilderness” space where nature, community and tourism could coexist. A few months later, the pandemic brought Surwahi’s plans to a standstill. The founders continued supporting the project through their corporate incomes. In December 2021, the ecoestate added two family suites named Jholar and Kariwah. Both were named after villages that were relocated outside Kanha in the 1970s when the national park was established. Maikal Express opened in October 2019 as an eight-bed earthen dormitory near Kanha. “We named them deliberately,” says Ankit, “to keep that memory alive.” Today, Surwahi is fully operational. It remains small by design and continues to develop each part of the property around its environmental and community goals. A toilet that uses banana trees to break down waste Once the buildings were ready, the team faced another difficult question: how could they run a hospitality property in a forest buffer zone without damaging its surroundings? They examined sanitation, water, energy and waste with the same care they had brought to the land and construction. Conventional septic tanks were ruled out from the beginning. Finding another solution in a remote forest-edge location required extensive research. “There was no local precedent,” says Ankit. “Every solution had to be researched and built from the ground up.” Jholar is one of two family suites added in 2021 and named after a relocated village. With support from Safalgram in Uttar Pradesh and international sanitation expert Marta Vanduzer-Snow from the USA, the team installed an Evapotranspiration Toilet. In this zero-discharge system, waste is broken down naturally with the help of banana trees and other nitrogen-loving plants. The team says it was the first system of its kind in Madhya Pradesh. A 1,500-litre bio-digester using bacteria developed by the Defence Research and Development Organisation sits on the other side of the property. According to the team, it was also the first system of its kind used by a hospitality property in the region at the time. Water received the same attention. “You don’t guess with groundwater in a forest,” says Ankit. “We believe we may be the first lodge in Madhya Pradesh to have done this before drilling.” Rainwater from the roof of Satkon, the dining space, is channelled into a fish pond within it. When the pond fills, the overflow helps recharge an old well nearby. Rainwater from Satkon’s roof is directed into a fish pond inside the dining space. The property also uses a chemical-free earthen plunge pool in place of a conventional swimming pool. Solar power runs the estate, while a wood-fired boiler provides hot water. Thin cotton towels reduce the water needed during each wash, and plastic is completely banned. The founders carried this thinking beyond the property boundary. In 2017, Ankit proposed building a stop dam on the nearby Sandook Stream so that farmers could retain water and grow a second crop. “It took years of persistent follow-ups across panchayat (village council), tehsil (administrative subdivision) and district levels before it was finally approved and built under MNREGA,” says Ankit. “That is the thing about doing the right thing in this country. You have to be willing to wait and keep showing up.” Surwahi’s buildings combine mud walls, reused roof tiles and locally made wooden features. The banks of the stop dam eroded after its first two monsoons. Since then, Surwahi’s staff and local residents have maintained it seasonally using sand-filled gunny bags and catchment dug-outs. The water now remains available for longer, supporting a second crop, fishing, cattle grazing and the foliage growing along the Sandook. At least 10 families benefit directly. Water alone, however, could not solve the farmers’ irrigation problem. They had access to water, but lacked electricity to pump it into their fields. In 2019, Surwahi funded and hosted an 11 kVA transformer and grid connection for the farmland. The team describes it as the first installation of its kind in Madhya Pradesh. With the grid line and subsidised electricity billing from the state, at least seven farmers began growing a second crop. Shared outdoor spaces sit alongside Surwahi’s dormitory and its two family suites. Surwahi’s work has received recognition from ICRT, TOFT, Outlook Traveller and the Wildlife Tourism Conclave, among others. The founders, however, continue to measure their progress by the work that remains. Their internal sustainability scorecard covers water conservation, clean air, chemical-free soil, waste segregation and recycling, toilet waste management, reforestation and community participation beyond direct employment. It also considers inclusive facilities for pets, older people and guests with disabilities, alignment with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, local sourcing of food and materials, and sustainable building design, including the earthen plunge pool. Against this scorecard, the team says Surwahi comfortably crosses the halfway mark and estimates that it currently operates at 70 to 80 percent sustainability. A shared work area provides desks, books and basic facilities for guests at the estate. “We currently operate at 70 to 80 percent sustainability,” says Ankit. “But our goal is to keep closing that gap and reach 100% soon.” The team sees reaching 90 percent and beyond as the next difficult phase of that work. When forests and people thrive together For all the work that went into restoring the land, Ankit felt that conservation had to improve the lives of those who lived around the forest. “If the forest is thriving but the people living around it are not, then we haven’t really solved anything,” says Ankit. “We wanted to change that.” The team began with a clear principle: hire locally, train people patiently and create opportunities that would last beyond individual jobs. Priyanka Rastogi, another travel industry veteran, joined the team to lead planning and strategy. Suraj Singh and Lokesh Dongre joined as interns in 2024 and soon grew into core roles focused on community-based tourism. Kariwah’s corridor mural depicts a monkey, elephants, birds and trees from the landscape. “Creating jobs is only the first step,” says Priyanka. “The real impact comes when people gain the confidence and skills to take ownership, grow into leadership roles, and build long-term careers.” A caretaker became manager, and a trainee became chef Narendra Patle from Sarekha village was one of Surwahi’s earliest hires. He joined as a caretaker while the ecoestate was still under construction. Hospitality was an unfamiliar field for him. With training and mentoring from Ankit, Narendra grew into the role of Estate Manager and now helps run Surwahi. Pradeep Uikey followed a similar path. He joined without any formal culinary training and now works as the ecoestate’s full-time chef. Pradeep Uikey joined without formal culinary training and now works as Surwahi’s chef. For Ankit, their journeys reflect what community-led tourism should achieve. “It would have been easier to bring in experienced managers from outside,” Priyanka says. “But then the knowledge leaves when they leave. When local people grow into leadership positions, the benefits stay within the community.” Some of Surwahi’s widest community work has developed through the women of neighbouring villages. In 2017, the founders helped establish the Deetya Jagriti Self-Help Group after noticing that local women had limited opportunities to earn independently or build a collective voice. Today, more than 40 women from nearby villages are part of the group. Through small member deposits, they have pooled over Rs 1 lakh. Jholar’s corridor carries hand-painted scenes of deer, birds and trees from the region. The group lends this money internally at one percent monthly interest, functioning as a small micro-finance body. Members have used the loans to buy seeds and cattle, and to meet urgent medical expenses and pay their children’s school fees. Surwahi is now exploring a partnership with a larger non-banking financial company or registration with the National or State Livelihood Mission to formalise the group’s work. It is also seeking local partners who can offer members practical skills training. The impact reaches people across the surrounding villages in different ways. “Neighbouring farmer Sohan Ji takes guests through his fields during the monsoon, explaining how crops are grown in the region,” says Suraj. Gond artist Sanjay Pancheshwar conducts painting workshops for guests, while potters from nearby Boda village meet visitors interested in their craft. “Bamboo artisans receive regular work through the property’s commitment to local sourcing,” adds Lokesh. Wildlife murals appear along the corridors connecting Surwahi’s family suites. Surwahi directly employs more than seven people from nearby villages. The founders estimate that over 50 others benefit through a wider network of women from Deetya Jagriti, farmers, artists, potters, bamboo workers, home hosts and families that supply produce to the property. For Ankit, the relationship between Surwahi and the surrounding communities matters as much as these numbers. “There is a very important difference between treating someone as a beneficiary and treating them as a partner,” he says. “A beneficiary receives. A partner contributes, grows and owns the story.” That approach shapes the meals served at the estate, the art and farming workshops offered to visitors, and the conversations guests have with people whose families have lived alongside the forest for generations. Ten years ago, Ankit and Pradeep began with a question that had followed them through their careers in tourism: could travel benefit a place as much as it benefits the traveller? The answer remains a work in progress. The forest continues to regenerate, local workers now lead the property, farmers are growing a second crop, and the women’s group is working towards a more formal community lending model. “Today, we are still working towards that answer,” says Ankit. Before you visit How to reach: Jabalpur Airport, 160 km away Tariffs: Double rooms from Rs 10,000, plus taxes Best time to visit: September to June For more details, visit their website .

items.title: The Scientist Who Fasted 111 Days for the Ganga. What Changed After His Death?
items.blurb: On the banks of the Ganga in Haridwar, an 86-year-old man sat quietly with a glass of honey water beside him. Day after day, he refused food. Eventually, he gave up water too. Around him, the river flowed on — slower in some stretches than it once had, interrupted by barrages, burdened by pollution, and at the heart of one of India's longest environmental battles. He called for a free-flowing Ganga River with no illegal mining and dams, as well as a Ganga Protection Management Act. Photograph: (indiawaterportal) For 111 days, environmentalist and former IIT professor G D Agrawal, who had by then taken sanyas and become Swami Gyan Swaroop Sanand, waited for a response he believed could still save India's most sacred river. It never came. On 11 October 2018, Agrawal died after suffering a cardiac arrest at AIIMS Rishikesh. His death brought one of India's longest environmental fasts to an end. But it also left behind a question that still resonates today: What happened to the demands he was willing to die for? A scientist who turned to faith Long before he became a saffron-clad monk, Guru Das Agrawal was one of India's leading environmental engineers. He earned a PhD in Environmental Engineering from the University of California, Berkeley, taught civil engineering at IIT Kanpur , served as the first Member-Secretary of the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), and spent decades studying India's rivers. Earlier in his career, he had worked on aspects of the Tehri Dam project. The experience later shaped his opposition to large hydropower projects in the fragile Himalayan region. Unlike many activists, Agrawal's campaign was rooted in both science and faith. He warned that excessive dam construction, diversion of river water, unchecked sand mining and pollution were disrupting the Ganga's natural flow. For him, a river could not survive if it was treated only as a source of electricity, irrigation or transport. In 2011, believing scientific reports alone could not inspire enough public action, he took sanyas in Varanasi under Swami Avimukteshwaranand and became Swami Gyan Swaroop Sanand. A decade of fasting for one river His final fast was not an isolated act. Between 2008 and 2018, hunger strikes became his principal form of protest. His first major fast began in Uttarkashi in 2008, demanding stronger protection for the Ganga. Government assurances persuaded him to end the protest, but when progress remained slow, he returned to fasting. In 2009, he launched another fast against the Loharinag Pala Hydroelectric Project on the Bhagirathi River. Daily rituals, cremations along the banks, and the immersion of religious offerings and idols introduce solid waste, chemicals, and human remains into the river, compounding broader environmental and industrial crises. Photograph: (Rajesh Kumar Singh/AP) By the 36th day, his health had deteriorated sharply. The government eventually suspended the project, marking one of the movement's biggest victories. His campaigns also contributed to reviews of projects such as Maneri and Bhairon Ghati and helped pave the way for the declaration of the Bhagirathi Eco-Sensitive Zone. Even so, Agrawal believed much more needed to be done. Speaking to The Guardian during one of his campaigns, he said, "This water is not ordinary water to a Hindu. It is a matter of life and death for the Hindu faith." The final 111 days On 22 June 2018, Agrawal began what would become his last fast at Matri Sadan Ashram in Haridwar. His demands were clear. He wanted a Ganga Protection Act, legal recognition for a Ganga Bhakti Parishad that would have a say in decisions affecting the river, a halt to proposed hydropower projects in the upper Ganga basin, and tougher action against illegal mining, sand extraction and activities that disrupted the river's natural flow. Throughout the fast, he continued writing letters to the country's highest authorities. He argued that protecting the Ganga meant restoring its natural flow, not simply cleaning its surface. For months, he survived mainly on honey and water. As his condition worsened, he stopped drinking water as well and refused intravenous support. On the 111th day of his fast, he died. Did his sacrifice change anything? The Ganga Protection Act that Agrawal envisioned was never passed in the form he had proposed. Yet one of the ideas he championed — that a healthy river needs uninterrupted flow, not just clean water — continues to shape conversations around river conservation. Today, many of those defending the Ganga are ordinary citizens who may never have met Agrawal but share his belief that rivers need people willing to protect them. In Patna, engineer Shubham Kumar started the Being Helper Foundation after beginning with solo Sunday clean-up drives along the riverbank. The initiative has since grown into a volunteer network of more than 1,100 people. According to the organisation, it has removed over 1.4 lakh kg of plastic waste, recycled more than 2 lakh plastic bottles, distributed thousands of dustbins, and expanded its work to cities including Varanasi and Ranchi. In Uttarakhand, Project Aviral works to reduce plastic pollution in Haridwar and Rishikesh by promoting waste segregation, supporting municipal waste management and encouraging residents and pilgrims to keep the river clean. As Ganga flows through major cities like Kanpur, Varanasi, and into West Bengal, pollution levels drastically increase, making the water unfit for bathing. Photograph: (Julie McCarthy/NPR) Scientists have also joined the effort. Earlier this year, researchers from Kolkata's Variable Energy Cyclotron Centre trekked 310 km from Gomukh to Haridwar, collecting plastic waste from remote Himalayan stretches, cleaning ghats and working with local communities to spread awareness about river pollution. Their expedition removed hundreds of kilograms of non-biodegradable waste while highlighting that pollution begins even near the glacier where the Ganga originates. None of these efforts alone fulfils the vision Agrawal spent a decade fighting for. They cannot replace the legal protections he sought or resolve every challenge facing the river. But together, they carry forward the spirit of his movement. His final protest was never only about one law. It was about reminding India that protecting the Ganga cannot be the government's responsibility alone. It also depends on scientists, volunteers, communities and ordinary citizens who believe a river is far more than the water it carries. It is a living ecosystem that sustains millions of lives, and its future depends on all of us. Sources: ‘Indian activist dies during hunger strike over Ganges river pollution’ : by Michael Safi, Published on 12 Oct 2018 ‘GD Agarwal (1932-2018): Ganga has lost its steadfast champion’ : by Soumya Sarkar, Published on Oct 11, 2018 'I cannot be a mute spectator to the carnage of Ganga' : by Soma Basu, Published on 13 Sep 2013
